Region concentration in East US (and secondary West US2):  many core services (storage accounts: 24.2% in eastus, virtual networks: 22.4% in eastus, SQL databases: 80% in eastus) show an eastus preference so plan the finance web app and its database in eastus (or westus2 as a secondary region) to align with existing resource placement and reduce cross-region latency and data egress.

WAF-enabled Application Gateways are a standard front-end choice:  all recorded application gateways use WAF_v2 with HTTPS listeners, indicating a tenant preference for inline web application protection, so place the internal finance app behind an internal Application Gateway (WAF_v2) to match operational patterns and enforce consistent WAF rules.

Private connectivity is already in use but mixed with public access:  the tenant has many private endpoints (56) and private DNS zones (74) alongside services that often allow public network access (Key Vaults publicnetworkaccess Enabled 92.3%, Storage defaultaction Allow 92.1%), so for a sensitive finance app explicitly design private endpoints + private DNS for the SQL backend and Key Vault and document the deliberate choice to disable public network access for those resources.

Key Vault usage shows soft-delete and mixed access controls:  most Key Vaults have soft delete enabled (98.8%) but RBAC authorization is disabled in 60.4% and public access is enabled in 92.3%, so for secrets used by the finance app adopt a hardened pattern (enable RBAC/disable broad public access and use private endpoint) even though tenant defaults often leave vaults publicly reachable.

Managed identities are commonly used:  there are 370 user-assigned managed identities across the tenant, so plan to use a user-assigned managed identity for the web app to authenticate to the SQL backend and Key Vault rather than embedding credentials, matching existing identity practices.

SQL deployment patterns favour vCore Hyperscale/Gen5 options and eastus placement:  among databases sampled 60% are Hyperscale and 80% are in eastus with Gen5 family common, so if the finance workload needs scaling consider a vCore Hyperscale (Gen5) database in eastus to align with existing performance/cost profiles.

Networking and perimeter defaults prefer Standard SKUs for platform services:  public IPs and load balancers are overwhelmingly Standard SKU (public IP Standard 95.9%, load balancers Standard 100%), so choose Standard SKU resources for internal load balancing and gateway components to match tenant-level networking configurations and SLAs.

Storage replication choices lean toward LRS but GRS/RAGRS are also used:  storage accounts are 48.5% Standard_LRS with 23.3% Standard_GRS and 17.2% RAGRS, so for the finance app keep LRS for cost-sensitive application storage where acceptable and explicitly evaluate GRS/RAGRS for backups or critical financial data where higher durability is required.

Network isolation is a frequent design decision:  there are 957 network security groups and many virtual networks with named subnets (default 40.5%, appSubnet 10.7%), so design the finance web app into an appSubnet protected by NSGs and leverage existing network manager constructs to enforce consistent security controls across resource groups.
